title: NHS — Palantir Federated Data Platform + COVID-19 Datastore

profile.vector: NHS — Palantir Federated Data Platform (FDP) + COVID-19 Data Store
profile.uk_body: NHS England (with downstream: 214 acute NHS trusts and all 42 Integrated Care Boards in England); contracting authority NHS England. Prior COVID-era counterpart: NHSX / NHS Digital.
profile.summary: Palantir is the prime contractor for NHS England's Federated Data Platform (FDP), a ~£330m, seven-year deal awarded November 2023 and built on Palantir Foundry. The FDP is an OPERATIONAL direct-care layer — it joins up trust-level data for waiting-list management, bed/capacity visibility, discharge planning, vaccination and population-health, with real-time clinical use. It evolved directly from the 2020 COVID-19 Data Store (originally a £1 Foundry deal, later a £23m extension), which Palantir built with Faculty, Google, Amazon and Microsoft. NHS England is ALSO a documented adopter of Splink (per Splink's own use-cases page) for STATISTICAL/research-grade record linkage, making NHS England the single strongest overlap of the investigation: an operational Palantir layer and a statistical Splink layer inside the same body. Critically, NO documented shared pipeline or data-flow ties Foundry/FDP to Splink — they are two separate workstreams co-located in one organisation, so the link is SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP, not a direct technical link.
profile.palantir_product: Palantir Foundry (data-integration / operational layer). Palantir's separate Gotham (defence/intelligence) product is NOT the NHS product, though Foundry-Gotham interoperability is a capability cited by critics. The FDP is explicitly Foundry-based.
what_palantir_does: Operates the NHS Federated Data Platform on Palantir Foundry, joining previously siloed NHS trust data into a unified operational view across 168 of 214 trusts signed up (123 live) and 41 of 42 ICBs (Greater Manchester the sole holdout, as of mid-2026).

Runs operational direct-care products: Inpatients/Outpatients Care Coordination (surgery waiting lists, theatre scheduling, prioritising 'those with the most urgent needs'), Referral-to-Treatment validation, near real-time bed visibility, discharge planning (OPTICA tool).

Five priority use cases: elective recovery, vaccination & immunisation, population health management, care coordination, supply-chain optimisation; plus a 'cloud-based data warehouse' use case for statutory operational management info.

Built and ran the 2020 NHS COVID-19 Data Store on Foundry — ingesting sensitive Covid patient data, hospital occupancy, ventilator/PPE supply, 999/111 calls, to feed dashboards and (with Faculty) modelling/forecasting of spread and resource impact.

As of mid-2026, NHS England created a new 'admin' role allowing some Palantir staff to access IDENTIFIABLE patient data in the NDIT environment — contradicting the DPIA that said only NHS staff would have such access (flagged by the National Data Guardian, 3 June 2026).
profile.link_to_splink_work: SAME BODY, SEPARATE STACKS — no proven shared pipeline. NHS England appears on Splink's maintainer-curated use-cases page as a Splink adopter (statistical/research-grade entity resolution on PDS/MPS-type data); separately, NHS England contracts Palantir to run the operational FDP on Foundry. These are two distinct layers in one organisation: Splink = statistical/research linkage (research-only framing, like MoJ Data First); Palantir/FDP = operational direct-care, real-time frontline triage. There is NO documented data-flow, contract, or technical integration connecting Splink output to Foundry/FDP, and I did not manufacture one. This matches the prior investigation's 'NHS = MIXED' verdict (Splink research linkage + FDP/Foundry operational direct-care). The shared theme — citizen data joined up without individual consent or opt-out — is the thematic through-line; the concrete tie is co-location in NHS England only.
profile.contract_value_dates: FDP: awarded 21 Nov 2023; up to £330m over seven years (Contracts Finder records an initial value of ~£182m, rising to £330m; year-one investment at least £25.6m). Published contract term Nov 2023–Feb 2027, with a Parliament decision due in 2026 on extension beyond the Feb 2027 break point. Consortium: Palantir (prime) with Accenture, PwC, NECS and Carnall Farrar. COVID-19 Data Store: original 2020 deal at a nominal £1 for Foundry; extended Dec 2020 via a two-year, £23m contract running to Dec 2022 (Faculty's parallel contract ~£930,000).
profile.operational_vs_statistical: OPERATIONAL. The FDP is a live direct-care/operational management platform (waiting-list triage, bed management, discharge, real-time clinical prioritisation), explicitly NOT a secondary-use research system in NHS England's framing. This is the key contrast with Splink's statistical/research-only positioning at NHS England — and it is why the consent/opt-out fight is so sharp.
profile.consent_oversight: The national data opt-out does NOT apply to the FDP because NHS England classifies FDP use as DIRECT CARE (the opt-out covers only secondary uses — planning/research). Result: individual patients cannot opt out of having their identifiable data processed in the Palantir-run platform; only their hospital/trust can decline to adopt FDP (e.g. Greater Manchester ICB). No individual consent or notification is documented — mirroring the Splink-investigation's central finding (lawful statutory/direct-care basis, but no individual consent/opt-out). Oversight: National Data Guardian (NDG) issued a statement 3 June 2026 after the 'Not With My NHS Data' campaign, saying it was not told that external contractor (Palantir) staff would access identifiable data in NDIT despite the DPIA stating only NHS staff would, and sought clarification. An internal NHS briefing reportedly warned the admin-role change risked 'loss of public confidence.'
controversies_litigation: 2021: openDemocracy (backed by Foxglove) brought a judicial review over the £23m COVID Data Store deal awarded without competition; the government CONCEDED (Apr 2021), committing not to expand Palantir's use to non-Covid purposes without public consultation/patient juries and a fresh data-protection analysis.

Nov 2023: Foxglove, with Just Treatment, the Doctors' Association UK and the National Pensioners Convention, launched legal action arguing there is NO lawful basis to proceed with the FDP without new primary legislation/Parliamentary explanation of how data flows comply with the law and the common-law duty of confidentiality.
Good Law Project sent a pre-action protocol letter over heavy redactions (~three-quarters of the contract text) in the published FDP contract.
Mid-2026: NHS England's new 'admin' role granting some Palantir staff access to identifiable patient data triggered the 'Not With My NHS Data' campaign and an NDG intervention (3 June 2026).

Broader controversy over Palantir's history with US ICE/border enforcement, defence/intelligence (Gotham), and Peter Thiel ties fuelling distrust of a US firm holding NHS data; re-identification risk of 'de-identified' health data raised by MP David Davis.
Reported low/slow uptake at some hospitals; Greater Manchester ICB the single ICB holdout.
datasets: NHS trust-level operational/clinical data: elective waiting lists, RTT pathways, theatre/surgery scheduling, inpatient/outpatient records, bed occupancy/capacity, discharge data (OPTICA)
Vaccination & immunisation data
Population health management datasets
Supply-chain/logistics data (PPE, stock)
COVID-era (Data Store): Covid test/case data, hospital occupancy, ventilator stock, 999/111 call data, A&E and bed-capacity feeds
Identifiable patient-level data at trust level (de-identified when shared nationally; identifiable accessible to some Palantir 'admin' staff in NDIT as of 2026)

profile.caveats: Tiering is deliberately SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP, NOT DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK: I found NO documented contract, data-flow, or technical integration connecting Splink to Palantir Foundry/FDP. NHS England's status as a Splink adopter rests on Splink's own maintainer-curated use-cases page (authoritative for adoption, but it does not detail which NHS team/dataset, nor connect to FDP). The £330m headline is a ceiling; the Contracts Finder award value to Palantir was reported nearer ~£182m, so 'up to £330m over seven years' is the accurate phrasing. The mid-2026 'admin role' / identifiable-data access detail and uptake figures (168/214 trusts, 41/42 ICBs) are as reported around June 2026 and may shift. Whether FDP processing is genuinely 'direct care' (and thus exempt from the national data opt-out) is the contested legal question at the heart of the Foxglove litigation — treat NHS England's 'direct care' classification as a contested claim, not settled fact.
unverified_flags: Exact current Palantir/FDP contract spend-to-date vs the £330m ceiling not independently confirmed from Contracts Finder in this pass
Specific NHS team/dataset using Splink (vs FDP) not pinned to a named record — relies on Splink use-cases page adoption listing
Whether the contract will be extended past the Feb 2027 break clause (Parliament decision pending in 2026)
Precise scope/permissions of the new Palantir 'admin' role beyond 'access to identifiable patient data in NDIT' as reported

overclaims: Contract term phrasing 'Published contract term Nov 2023–Feb 2027' is imprecise and overclaims a hard end date. The verified structure is 3+2+1+1: a committed initial ~3-year term (ending ~March 2027), extendable to a max 7 years / ceiling end ~March 2031. February 2027 is when the BREAK CLAUSE becomes available, not a contract end date. Reword to: 'committed 3-year term, break clause available Feb 2027, max term to ~March 2031.'

The Splink adoption claim is HEDGED MORE WEAKLY than the evidence supports — but in the safe direction, so not a publication risk. The profile says NHS England's Splink status 'rests on Splink's own maintainer-curated use-cases page' and is unspecific. In fact NHS England Data Science runs a named, documented 'Data Linkage Hub' (nhsengland.github.io/datascience) actively implementing a Splink probabilistic linkage model. This is a stronger, NHS-first-party source than the profile credits.

Minor: 'real-time clinical use' / 'real-time frontline triage' phrasing is NHS England's framing of FDP as direct care, which is the contested question in the Foxglove litigation. The profile does caveat this, but the body summary states it more confidently ('OPERATIONAL... real-time clinical prioritisation') than the contested legal status warrants. Already flagged in caveats, acceptable.
Faculty parallel contract value stated as '~£930,000'; sources confirm 'more than £1m' / ~£1m. The £930k figure is plausible but should carry a citation or be softened to '~£1m'.
strongest_sourced_claims: FDP awarded 21 Nov 2023 to Palantir-led consortium; up to £330m over seven years, with Contracts Finder initial value ~£182m (the £330m is a non-committed forecast ceiling). Consortium: Palantir (prime), Accenture, PwC, NECS, Carnall Farrar. Verified against Contracts Finder notice + NHS England contract explainer + multiple outlets.

COVID-19 Data Store: original ~£1 emergency Foundry deal (March 2020), extended via £23m contract commencing 12 Dec 2020 running to Dec 2022. Verified (Digital Health, openDemocracy, Foxglove, Computer Weekly).

2021 openDemocracy/Foxglove judicial review over the £23m deal; government conceded April 2021, committing not to expand Palantir use to non-Covid purposes without public consultation. Verified (Foxglove, openDemocracy, The Register).

National Data Guardian statement 3 June 2026 (Dr Nicola Byrne) confirming DPIA said identifiable-data access limited to NHS staff, but external Palantir contractor staff (3 engineers, admin role in NDIT) also have access; NHS England conceded a DPIA error. Verified (GOV.UK NDG statement, Digital Health, Computing, NHS England response page).
Uptake: 168 of 214 trusts signed up (123 live), 41 of 42 ICBs on board, Greater Manchester the sole ICB holdout (NHS England uptake stats, updated March 2026). Verified.
FDP is built on Palantir Foundry, distinct from Gotham; verified against NHS England and reporting.

NHS England operates a Splink-based probabilistic linkage workstream (Data Linkage Hub) with NO documented connection to Palantir/Foundry/FDP — the source page does not mention Palantir, Foundry, or the FDP at all. This DIRECTLY corroborates the profile's central honest claim that the two are separate stacks co-located in one body.
unsupported_to_remove: Remove or correct the hard 'Nov 2023–Feb 2027' contract-term phrasing — replace with the verified 3+2+1+1 structure (committed ~3yr to ~March 2027, break clause from Feb 2027, ceiling ~March 2031).
Pin the Faculty '~£930,000' figure to a citation or soften to '~£1m'; otherwise remove the precise number.
corrections: Contract term: not a flat Nov 2023–Feb 2027 deal. It is a 3+2+1+1 incremental structure — committed initial term ~3 years (first decision point ~March 2027), break clause available from Feb 2027, maximum term to ~March 2031. The Parliament/DHSC extension decision in 2026/27 concerns triggering the first extension past the committed term.

Strengthen the Splink-adoption sourcing: cite NHS England Data Science 'Data Linkage Hub' (nhsengland.github.io/datascience/our_work/data-linkage-hub) as the first-party source, not only Splink's own use-cases page. Note it frames Splink for both business-process linkage and research on admin data, and crucially makes no reference to Palantir/Foundry/FDP.
verification.notes: Adversarial conclusion: the SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP tier is CORRECT and defensible — I tried to find a documented Splink-to-Foundry/FDP data flow, contract, or technical integration and found none. The NHS England Data Linkage Hub (the strongest Splink source) explicitly does not mention Palantir, Foundry, or the FDP, confirming the profile's honest 'separate stacks, no shared pipeline' framing rather than refuting it. The profile resists the temptation to inflate to DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK and explicitly states it did not manufacture a tie — this is correct discipline. As a Palantir lawyer, the only fair pushbacks are: (1) the imprecise contract end-date phrasing (genuine overclaim, must fix); (2) the contested 'direct care' classification stated too confidently in the body (already caveated, acceptable); (3) the unsourced Faculty £930k figure. None of these undermine the connection; all are correctable. The Splink adjacency is real (same body, NHS England) but purely thematic/co-locational at the technical level — the profile already labels it as such. SOLID, publishable after the two corrections above.

profile.uk_body: UK Ministry of Defence (MoD), including Defence Digital, Royal Navy, and the wider UK Armed Forces; partnership signed at MoD/HM Government level (Sept 2025). Adjacent: Office for Veterans' Affairs / ONS for the Splink-side linkage.
profile.summary: Palantir has a deep, long-standing and well-documented relationship with the UK MoD via Foundry: a £75m Enterprise Agreement (Dec 2022), a September 2025 strategic partnership worth up to £750m over five years (with a Palantir £1.5bn UK investment commitment and a London European-defence HQ), and a £240.6m three-year follow-on enterprise agreement directly awarded on 30 Dec 2025 (start 1 Apr 2026) under a defence/security exemption with no competition. Foundry ingests disparate MoD data into an ontology to support strategic, tactical and "live operational decision making" across classifications, NATO-interoperable. The MoD is separately a documented Splink user — but the Splink linkage is ONS statistical work on the MoD Service Leavers Database↔2011 Census, and the operational Veterans Card verification system, neither of which is documented as flowing through Palantir Foundry. There is NO documented Palantir↔Splink shared pipeline; the connection is co-residence in the same department, not a proven joined data flow, hence SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP.
what_palantir_does: Foundry 'digests' disparate MoD data sources, cleans/harmonises them and maps them to an ontology (objects, properties, relationships) rather than rows/columns, treating defence data as a strategic asset across all operational domains, Top Level Budgets and UK Armed Forces bases.

Supports advanced scenario planning, hypothesis testing, and real-time decision-making from headquarters to the front line; the £240.6m follow-on explicitly covers 'critical strategic, tactical and live operational decision making across classifications' interoperable with NATO and allied nations' Palantir systems.
Royal Navy uses Foundry for operational planning, logistics and fleet availability; Foundry was used to reorganise/streamline personnel during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Acts as the underlying MoD 'data fabric' — e.g. Hadean's £20m simulation/AI products integrate with the MoD data fabric underpinned by Palantir Foundry (Sept 2025).
No-code/low-code app builders let MoD personnel build tools on the integrated data, democratising access across Defence Digital's 'integrated digital enterprise'.
MoD/ministers state UK defence data remains sovereign, resides in the UK, and cannot be changed without UK consent (contractual control), per the Feb 2026 Lords debate.
profile.link_to_splink_work: SAME DEPARTMENT, NO PROVEN SHARED PIPELINE. MoD is a genuine Splink-side entity in two ways: (1) ONS used Splink 2 (the MoJ tool) to probabilistically link the MoD-supplied Service Leavers Database (UK service leavers 1975-2022; 1,603,782 deduplicated person-records) plus MoD's Compensation and Pensions System geography to the 2011 Census — a statistical/research linkage (~40% / 649,186 matched); (2) the MoD/Office for Veterans' Affairs Veterans Card system uses Splink to verify applicants against historic service records (operational, but a discrete verification function). Palantir Foundry is the MoD's broader operational data platform. Crucially, there is NO documented integration, data flow, or shared pipeline between Foundry and the Splink-based linkage — searches for Foundry/Splink integration return only generic Foundry ontology docs. So Palantir and Splink coexist inside the MoD but the Splink Service-Leavers/Census linkage is an ONS statistical product and the Veterans Card a separate system; do NOT assert that veterans/service-leaver linkage runs through Palantir. Trajectory/theme overlap (entity resolution on MoD person data) is real; a technical link is not documented.
profile.palantir_product: Palantir Foundry (MoD data platform / 'data fabric', ontology). Distinct from Palantir Gotham (Palantir's defence/intelligence operational product) and from the NHS FDP and Gotham deployments referenced for context.
profile.contract_value_dates: £75m Enterprise Agreement, 3 years, announced 21 Dec 2022 (digital transformation/data exploitation across Defence). September 2025 strategic partnership: framework worth up to £750m over five years, with Palantir committing to invest £1.5bn in the UK and base its European defence arm in London (~350 jobs). £240.6m follow-on enterprise agreement directly awarded 30 Dec 2025, 3-year term starting 1 Apr 2026, awarded WITHOUT competition under a defence-and-security exemption (redacted contract available ~90 days post-award on request). Related: Palantir/Hadean £20m UK military AI/simulation deal (Sept 2025).
profile.operational_vs_statistical: PALANTIR SIDE = OPERATIONAL: Foundry explicitly supports 'live operational decision making' across strategic/tactical levels, Royal Navy logistics/fleet availability, and NATO-interoperable operations — operational by design, not statistical. SPLINK SIDE = MIXED: the Service Leavers Database↔2011 Census linkage is STATISTICAL/research (ONS, characterising the veteran population), while the Veterans Card verification is OPERATIONAL but narrow (eligibility/identity verification for a benefit/ID card), and neither is documented as running on Palantir.
profile.consent_oversight: The ONS Service Leavers↔Census linkage methodology does not document individual consent/notification/opt-out (consistent with statutory public-task statistical linkage, no individual consent). MoD/ministers assert contractual oversight over Foundry: data sovereignty (resides in UK, under MoD control, no changes without UK consent). Parliamentary oversight is weak on procurement: the £240.6m award bypassed competition via a defence/security exemption; the Feb 2026 Lords debate (Baroness Goldie, Lord Fox, Baroness Cash) pressed on excluding British competitors, outsourcing a 'sovereign capability', and political-conflict concerns. No published minutes from the PM's Feb 2025 visit to Palantir HQ.
controversies_litigation: £240.6m award made without competitive tender via defence/security exemption; British firms reportedly interested but not allowed to compete (Lords debate, Feb 2026).
Revolving-door concerns: Palantir hired four former MoD officials in 2025, including ex-policy director Barnaby Kistruck (subject to business-appointment restrictions).
Political-conflict allegations aired in the Lords: PM's Feb 2025 visit to Palantir HQ (former ambassador/figure Peter Mandelson linked), and a Labour donation question (Baroness Cash).

Broader Palantir reputational/ethics controversy: UN Special Rapporteur and human-rights groups allege complicity in Israel's AI-targeting in Gaza (Jan 2024 IDF strategic partnership); Amnesty/Medact campaign to terminate the separate £330m NHS FDP contract; Liberty (June 2025) flagged Palantir trials with UK police; German GFF constitutional complaints over Gotham predictive-policing. These attach to Palantir as a vendor, not specifically to a documented MoD data-misuse finding.
Data-sovereignty/outsourcing critique (Lord Fox): risk of outsourcing not just data but the capability to use it.
datasets: MoD operational/Defence data across all operational domains ingested into Foundry (capability/logistics/personnel/planning) — specifics classified
Royal Navy operational planning, logistics and fleet/maintenance data
MoD personnel data (Foundry used to reorganise personnel during COVID-19)
SPLINK-SIDE (not Palantir): MoD Service Leavers Database (service leavers 1975-2022) + MoD Compensation and Pensions System, linked by ONS to 2011 Census
SPLINK-SIDE (not Palantir): Veterans Card applicant data vs historic service records
unverified_flags: No public evidence that the Splink Service-Leavers/Census linkage or Veterans Card verification runs on, or feeds, Palantir Foundry — treat any such claim as unverified.
Exact datasets inside MoD Foundry are classified/undisclosed; 'all operational domains' is the public characterisation only.
£1.5bn is a Palantir investment COMMITMENT, not contracted MoD spend; the contracted framework ceiling is up to £750m/5yrs.
Gaza/ICE/police controversies are vendor-level reputational issues; no documented finding that MoD Foundry has been used for unlawful targeting or surveillance of UK citizens.

Computing.co.uk and The Nation/Al Jazeera pieces were not all directly fetched (some 403); figures cross-checked against PRNewswire, The Register, TechRadar, Hansard/ParallelParliament and ONS primary sources.

profile.caveats: Confidence is high on the Palantir-MoD contract facts (multiple primary/serious sources agree on £75m/2022, £750m+£1.5bn/Sept 2025, £240.6m/30 Dec 2025) and on the existence of the MoD Splink touchpoints (ONS SLD↔Census; Veterans Card). The deliberately conservative judgement is the TIER: SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP, not DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK, because no source documents Foundry and Splink sharing a pipeline or data flow. The Splink Service-Leavers linkage is an ONS statistical product; the Veterans Card is a separate operational verification system. Resisting a manufactured technical link is the point of the brief. Some secondary pages (Computing, The Nation) returned 403 and were corroborated via other outlets rather than fetched directly.

overclaims: The £240.6m follow-on 'explicitly covers \'critical strategic, tactical and live operational decision making across classifications\'' is presented as a verbatim quote, but the cited Register article (theregister.com/2026/01/28) does not contain that exact wording in the fetched text. The phrase must be attributed to its true source (contract notice / Hansard) or softened to a paraphrase before publication.

Foundry was used to 'reorganise/streamline personnel during the COVID-19 pandemic' is stated as fact but is NOT supported by the cited PRNewswire 2022 release (which contains no COVID/personnel mention). This claim currently has no citation.

Hadean's '£20m simulation/AI products integrate with the MoD data fabric underpinned by Palantir Foundry' could not be confirmed — the cited BusinessWire URL failed to fetch (socket closed), so the £20m figure and the explicit 'underpinned by Palantir Foundry' integration assertion are presently uncorroborated from the cited source.

Political-conflict specifics (Peter Mandelson link to the PM's Feb 2025 Palantir HQ visit; the Labour donation question attributed to Baroness Cash) are stated with attributed names but were not independently confirmed against the Hansard/ParallelParliament primary text in this verification; they rest on a secondary characterisation.
strongest_sourced_claims: £75m three-year Enterprise Agreement (Dec 2022), including Foundry ontology and Royal Navy ('Palantir Foundry is critical to the Royal Navy's transformation', improved ship/submarine availability) — confirmed verbatim against PRNewswire 301707783.

£240.6m three-year follow-on, 30 Dec 2025 award, 1 Apr 2026 start, direct award under a defence-and-security exemption with no competition, plus the four 2025 revolving-door hires incl. ex-policy-director Barnaby Kistruck joining days after leaving MoD — confirmed against The Register (2026-01-28).

September 2025 strategic partnership: up to £750m over five years, Palantir £1.5bn UK investment commitment, London designated European defence HQ, ~350 jobs — confirmed against GOV.UK press release and multiple corroborating outlets (Bloomberg, Register, Computing).

ONS Service Leavers Database -> 2011 Census linkage uses Splink 2 (MoJ, Fellegi-Sunter); 1,603,782 deduplicated SLD person-records, 649,186 linked records, 40.5% link rate; Compensation and Pensions System used for geography — confirmed verbatim against the ONS methodology page, which makes NO mention of Palantir or Foundry.
MoD Veterans Card system uses Splink to verify applicants against historic service records — independently corroborated via GOV.UK / MoJ Splink case-study material.
unsupported_to_remove: Remove or re-cite the COVID-19 personnel reorganisation claim until a source other than the 2022 PRNewswire release supports it.

Remove the verbatim quotation marks around 'critical strategic, tactical and live operational decision making across classifications' unless the exact source string is cited; otherwise render as paraphrase.
Down-qualify the Hadean '£20m ... underpinned by Palantir Foundry' line to 'unverified (cited source not fetched)' until the BusinessWire page (or an equivalent) is successfully retrieved.
Either confirm via Hansard or hedge the Mandelson and Labour-donation political-conflict specifics; do not present named-individual allegations as documented without the primary debate text.
corrections: No tier change required: SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP is correct and appropriately conservative. The profile already refuses to assert a Foundry<->Splink data flow, and independent checks confirm that refusal is justified — the ONS SLD<->Census methodology names Splink 2 only and never mentions Palantir/Foundry. Do NOT let any editor upgrade this to DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK.

The Lords debate citation is dated 2026-02-10 (Hansard) / 2026-02-11 (ParallelParliament) and the body labels it the 'Feb 2026 Lords debate'; the Hansard URL path says 'commons' but the debate is described as House of Lords — verify chamber labelling for consistency before publication.

Consider adding the corroborating ONS 'Veterans' Survey 2022 to Census 2021 linkage' (also Splink-based) as a further Splink-side MoD touchpoint surfaced during verification — it strengthens, not weakens, the same-department framing.
verification.notes: Adversarial verification against primary sources (PRNewswire, The Register, GOV.UK, ONS methodology, MoJ Splink material) substantiates every load-bearing contract figure and the core analytical judgement. The central thesis — that Palantir Foundry and Splink merely co-reside inside the MoD with NO documented shared pipeline — survives hostile scrutiny: the ONS SLD<->Census document names Splink 2 and never mentions Palantir/Foundry, and no source documents a Foundry<->Splink integration. The £1.5bn is correctly flagged as an investment commitment, not contracted spend. Remaining issues are sourcing-hygiene overclaims (a possibly-misattributed verbatim quote, an uncited COVID claim, an unfetched Hadean source, and unconfirmed named political allegations) rather than structural errors. Fix those four items and the profile is publication-ready at the stated tier.

title: Home Office — Palantir (immigration, borders, law enforcement)

profile.vector: Home Office / immigration, borders, law enforcement — Palantir
profile.uk_body: Cabinet Office (border & customs data programme, the one documented borders contract); UK policing consortium (Bedfordshire/Hertfordshire/Cambridgeshire "Nectar" pilot + Leicestershire/East Midlands); Home Office immigration control as legal-exposure/capability frame (NOT a confirmed direct Palantir customer); NHS England FDP (cross-departmental capability concern). DPA 2018 immigration exemption governs Home Office data-subject rights.
profile.palantir_product: Foundry (Cabinet Office border/customs data integration; NHS FDP) and Gotham (defence/police investigative platform; the police "Nectar" pilot). Foundry and Gotham interoperate ("drag and drop") which is the basis of the cross-departmental concern.
profile.summary: Palantir holds a documented £27m Cabinet Office contract (awarded August 2020, no competitive tender) to process UK border and customs data, pitched explicitly around "risk-based monitoring for the movement of goods and people across the UK border" — a direct, documented borders/immigration-adjacent engagement facilitated by an introduction from former MI6 chief Sir John Sawers (Newbridge Advisory) to the Cabinet Office permanent secretary in July 2019. Separately, Palantir is building a real-time policing data platform (the "Nectar" pilot) for an East-of-England consortium plus a Leicestershire/East Midlands project, ingesting sensitive personal data including political opinions, health records and union membership — connecting it to the UK-policing entity in the Splink investigation. Critically, NO documented direct contract between Palantir and the Home Office for immigration ENFORCEMENT was found: the heavily-reported ImmigrationOS deportation platform is a US ICE contract, not UK. The UK Home Office immigration-enforcement link is therefore a CAPABILITY-and-trajectory concern (Foundry/Gotham cross-departmental interop, contingent on a future legal change such as Reform UK's pledged automatic Home Office/NHS/HMRC/DVLA/banks/police data-sharing), all operating against the backdrop of the DPA 2018 immigration exemption that the Court of Appeal has ruled unlawful (2021, then again 2023).
what_palantir_does: Cabinet Office border & customs (£27m, Aug 2020): integrates and analyses border/customs data to enable 'risk-based monitoring for the movement of goods and people across the UK border' post-Brexit — the firm's own pitch language. This is real, operational data processing about people crossing the UK border, the closest documented Palantir-to-borders link.

UK policing 'Nectar' pilot (Beds/Herts/Cambs consortium, ~9 forces initially, plus a separate Leicestershire-led East Midlands project): builds individual profiles by fusing crime records with intelligence, ingesting special-category data — political opinions, philosophical beliefs, health records, trade-union membership, sexual orientation, race, financial data — and data on victims, children, witnesses and vulnerable people. Stated to 'assist with decision making' and 'aid prevention, detection and investigation of crimes.' No contract value disclosed; early-stage pilot as of June 2025, with planned national expansion.

NHS England Federated Data Platform (£330m, 7 years, awarded Nov 2023): operational direct-care data platform. The immigration relevance is a CAPABILITY/CONCERN only — Palantir's interoperable Foundry/Gotham could, if the law changed, 'enable UK government departments such as the Home Office and police to more easily access patient data' (Medact briefing). No documented NHS-to-Home-Office enforcement data flow exists today.

Immigration ENFORCEMENT (deportation tooling): documented only in the US — ICE's ImmigrationOS ($30m, awarded April 2025, sole-source, prototype due Sep 2025, runs to Sep 2027) to identify/apprehend removal targets and track self-deportations in near-real-time. This is the model critics fear could be replicated in the UK, but there is NO UK Home Office equivalent contract on record.
profile.contract_value_dates: Cabinet Office border/customs: £27m, awarded August 2020, direct award / no competitive tender (introductory meeting July 2019). UK police Nectar pilot: value undisclosed, proof-of-concept stage as of June 2025. NHS FDP (context): £330m, 7 years, Nov 2023. MoD Enterprise Agreement (context, separate vector): £240.6m, 3 years, signed 30 Dec 2025, started 1 Apr 2026. Aggregate UK public-sector estimates: investigations cite 34+ contracts across 10+ departments totalling £670m+, exceeding £900m after the latest MoD deal. US ICE ImmigrationOS (NOT UK): $30m, April 2025, through Sep 2027.
profile.operational_vs_statistical: OPERATIONAL throughout. The Cabinet Office border/customs work is operational risk-monitoring of people/goods movement, not statistical research. The police Nectar platform is operational law-enforcement profiling and decision-support. NHS FDP is operational direct-care. None of these are research/statistical linkage like MoJ Data First. This is a key contrast with the statistics-vs-operations framing of the Splink investigation: Palantir sits firmly on the operational side.
profile.consent_oversight: No individual consent, notification or opt-out documented for any use; lawful basis asserted as public task / statutory function. The police DPIA itself recorded expected public 'skepticism or resistance' and significant breach consequences. For the Home Office, data-subject rights are curtailed by the DPA 2018 immigration exemption (Sch 2 para 4), ruled an unlawful GDPR Art 23(2) derogation by the Court of Appeal in 2021 ([2021] EWCA Civ 800) and found STILL unlawful by the High Court in 2023 — so any Palantir-run Home Office immigration platform would inherit a legally contested rights-suspension regime with weak oversight.
controversies_litigation: Procurement: £27m Cabinet Office border contract awarded without competitive tender; MI6-chief introduction (Sawers/Newbridge Advisory) drew MP criticism over transparency and lobbying-by-proxy (Sawers says his firm 'does not undertake lobbying').
Police Nectar pilot: Amnesty International says it 'violates people's right to privacy'; critics warn of 'dystopian predictive policing' and 'indiscriminate mass surveillance' (Liberty Investigates).

NHS FDP: sustained campaign (Medact, Foxglove, The Lowdown) over Palantir's ICE ties and the risk that interoperable architecture enables future immigration data-sharing; flagged in a Palantir shareholder PX14A6G SEC filing.
DPA 2018 immigration exemption ruled unlawful twice (Court of Appeal 2021; High Court 2023) — direct legal exposure for any Home Office data platform.
US precedent: ACLU and others document Palantir/ICE ImmigrationOS as core to the Trump-era removal campaign — the reputational and operational template the UK concern is built on.
datasets: UK border and customs data (movement of goods and people) — Cabinet Office
Police crime records, intelligence, and special-category personal data (political opinions, beliefs, health, union membership, sexual orientation, race, financial) — Nectar policing pilot
NHS patient/health data — Federated Data Platform (operational direct-care; immigration use is hypothetical/capability-only)
(US, not UK) ICE immigration and removal datasets — ImmigrationOS
profile.link_to_splink_work: PARTIAL OVERLAP, NOT A SHARED PIPELINE. (1) The Splink investigation found the Home Office link to be 'OPERATIONAL but HISTORICAL' (2017 NHS Digital–Home Office MoU, withdrawn Nov 2018) and noted Foundry/Gotham 'drag and drop' interop is CAPABILITY only — this research confirms and extends that: Palantir's documented UK borders contract is Cabinet Office, not Home Office, and no Palantir Home Office immigration-enforcement contract exists. (2) UK policing is a named Splink-investigation entity (Splink runs a daily North Essex PNC→police feed per moj-splink-master-record); Palantir's Nectar pilot operates in the SAME domain (East-of-England policing) but there is NO evidence Splink and Palantir share data or a pipeline — they are independent tools in the same departmental space = SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP for policing. (3) NHS England appears in both (Splink for research linkage; Palantir FDP for operational care) but again no shared pipeline. CRITICAL: do NOT manufacture a Splink↔Palantir technical link — none is documented. Palantir's distinct contribution is being the OPERATIONAL platform layer over which the statistics-vs-operations and DPA-immigration-exemption legal-exposure arguments would actually bite.

profile.caveats: High confidence on the documented £27m Cabinet Office border/customs contract, the police Nectar pilot, the NHS FDP, and the DPA 2018 immigration-exemption rulings. The borders work is contracted via the Cabinet Office; the immigration-crackdown scenario is a forward-looking CAPABILITY concern contingent on legal change, not an existing data flow. Remove the PX14A6G SEC-filing claim unless a primary EDGAR link is added.
verification.notes:
DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK tier is JUSTIFIED and should stand — but specifically and only on the basis of the £27m Cabinet Office BORDER/CUSTOMS contract and the Nectar POLICING pilot, both of which are documented, operational, and verified. The tier is NOT justified for Home Office immigration ENFORCEMENT, and the profile itself correctly and repeatedly downgrades that to a CAPABILITY/trajectory concern (no UK contract; ImmigrationOS is US ICE). That discipline is the profile's strongest feature and survives hostile review.

Splink<->Palantir technical/data link: I attempted to refute this and FOUND NOTHING TO REFUTE — the profile makes no such claim. It explicitly states 'PARTIAL OVERLAP, NOT A SHARED PIPELINE', 'do NOT manufacture a Splink<->Palantir technical link — none is documented', and classes the policing overlap as SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP and the NHS overlap as same-space-but-no-shared-pipeline. This is exactly the correct characterisation: Splink (MoJ research-linkage library, runs an operational North Essex PNC->police feed per the project's own moj-splink-master-record) and Palantir's Nectar operate in the same East-of-England policing domain as independent tools. No documented data flow, joint deployment, or interoperability between Splink and Palantir exists. The Foundry<->Gotham 'drag and drop' interop is internal to Palantir's own product suite and is correctly flagged as CAPABILITY only — it is NOT a Splink linkage. As a hostile critic I confirm: the profile does not overclaim the Splink link, and nothing in it needs downgrading on that axis.

Net: a well-disciplined, properly-hedged profile. The only publication blockers are citation hygiene (the misattributed aggregate figure and the unsupported SEC-filing reference), not the substance of the connection. Operational-vs-statistical framing is sound and correctly places Palantir on the operational side, in deliberate contrast to Splink's MoJ Data First research-only datasets.

title: UK policing / law enforcement — Palantir Gotham/Foundry

profile.vector: UK policing / law enforcement — Palantir Gotham/Foundry (operational crime intelligence + national licensing records)
profile.uk_body: UK police forces and national policing bodies: Bedfordshire, Hertfordshire, Cambridgeshire Police (Eastern Region "Nectar" pilot); Leicestershire Police / East Midlands Special Operations Unit (EMSOU); Metropolitan Police Service (blocked deal); National Police Chiefs' Council (NPCC) + Police Digital Service + Home Office (firearms/explosives/poisons licensing for 43 forces). IMPORTANT: NDAS (National Data Analytics Solution, West Midlands Police lead) is Accenture/AWS, NOT Palantir — do not conflate.
profile.summary: Palantir is embedded across UK operational policing via two confirmed regional pilots and a national licensing contract. The Eastern Region "Nectar" project (Bedfordshire/Hertfordshire/Cambridgeshire, initially fusing data from nine forces, with a stated ambition to "eventually apply it nationally") and an East Midlands project led by Leicestershire/EMSOU (~£818,750) build a "single, unified view" of citizen data. In June 2026 Palantir also won a ~£9m (up to 10-year; tender valued £17m incl. tax) NPCC/Police Digital Service/Home Office contract to run firearms, explosives-precursor and poisons licensing across all 43 forces in England and Wales. In May 2026 Sadiq Khan's office blocked a Met Police deal worth £25.3m (2026-27) plus a £24.8m extension (~£50m); Palantir signalled a legal challenge. These are OPERATIONAL crime-intelligence/decision-support systems processing 11 categories of special-category data on suspects, victims, children and witnesses, with DPIAs admitting no individual opt-out and no completed public consultation. The link to the Splink-investigation entities is a SAME-DEPARTMENT / geographic-trajectory overlap: Palantir's flagship Nectar pilot sits in the SAME East-of-England geography as the MoJ Splink "Master Record" daily North Essex PNC→police feed, but there is NO documented shared pipeline and no documented Palantir–PNC integration. The connection is real-but-parallel, not a proven data flow.
what_palantir_does: Eastern Region 'Nectar' pilot (Bedfordshire + Hertfordshire + Cambridgeshire, scaling to nine forces): integrates dozens of policing databases into a single AI-assisted platform to create detailed digital profiles and a 'single, unified view' / 'single source of truth' on suspects, victims, witnesses, children and vulnerable people; combines crime records with financial information and other intelligence; designed as a real-time data-sharing network to 'assist with decision making' and 'aid in the prevention, detection and investigation of crimes' — i.e. live operational use, not statistics.

Processes 11 types of special-category data: race, religion, political opinions, sex life/sexual orientation, health, trade-union membership, philosophical beliefs, plus social media posts, dating profiles and financial records (Bedfordshire DPIA, reported by Bedford Independent / Liberty Investigates).

Nectar 'Romanian fraud gang' case (Eastern Region Special Operations Unit, ERSOU): Palantir's Nectar processed 1.4TB from ~24 seized phones, machine-translated 100,000+ messages from Romanian, mapped suspect connections, tracked movements and flagged ~120 offences; ~6-9 men jailed (Nov 2024). Operational evidence-processing at scale.

East Midlands project (Leicestershire Police / EMSOU, ~£818,750): 'intelligence and investigation platform' fusing data from five East Midlands forces; contract listing reportedly removed from public record after press inquiries.

National firearms/explosives/poisons licensing system (NPCC + Police Digital Service + Home Office, ~£9m, up to 10 years, tender £17m incl. tax, announced June 2026): holds and manages how police grant/renew/revoke firearms, explosives-precursor and poisons records across all 43 forces in England and Wales, with 'integration with partner agencies and national systems' (potential expansion to Police Scotland and PSNI). This is a national operational person-level records system.

Metropolitan Police (blocked): proposed Palantir software to automate elements of criminal intelligence analysis to speed investigations; £25.3m (2026-27) + £24.8m extension (~£50m) — blocked May 2026 by the Deputy Mayor for Policing and Crime on procurement/value-for-money grounds (Met treated Palantir as sole supplier, no formal procurement strategy).

Palantir publicly DENIED its UK police software is used for predictive policing; critics (Amnesty, TUC, StopWatch, Liberty Investigates) warn it facilitates mass surveillance and 'dystopian predictive policing'.
profile.link_to_splink_work: SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP / geographic-trajectory only — NOT a documented shared pipeline. (1) Same domain: Palantir and MoJ Splink both operate in UK criminal justice / policing. (2) Striking geographic adjacency: the MoJ Splink 'Master Record' runs a daily North Essex PNC→police feed (per moj-splink-master-record, 6 Oct 2025, logged in project_splink_investigation), and Palantir's flagship operational pilot 'Nectar' is the EASTERN region network (Bedfordshire/Hertfordshire/Cambridgeshire/Essex-adjacent ERSOU) — same East-of-England operational policing space, same kind of person-level identity-resolution goal ('single unified view' / 'Master Record'/'Core Person Record'). (3) BUT: no document shows PNC or Splink-linked records flowing INTO Palantir Nectar, and no document shows Palantir integrating with the PNC. NDAS (the other big UK police-analytics programme, which DOES involve national fusion) is Accenture/AWS, not Palantir. The shared theme is the trajectory the Splink investigation already flagged — operational person-level record-linkage in policing with no individual consent/opt-out — now appearing via a SECOND, commercial vendor (Palantir) in the same geography. Treat the overlap as parallel/convergent, not as one feeding the other. Do NOT manufacture a Splink→Palantir technical link.
profile.palantir_product: Primarily the Gotham/Nectar investigative lineage (Gotham is Palantir's law-enforcement/intelligence platform; 'Nectar' is the branded UK police deployment) with Foundry-style data-integration underneath; the firearms-licensing records platform is a Foundry-class data/records system. UK police FOI refusals mean the exact product naming per force is often undisclosed; 'Nectar' is the confirmed UK police brand.
profile.contract_value_dates: Leicestershire/EMSOU: ~£818,750 (listing later removed from public record). Met Police (blocked May 2026): £25.3m for 2026-27 + £24.8m optional extension 2027-28 (~£50m, two-year). National firearms/explosives/poisons licensing (NPCC/Police Digital Service/Home Office, published 4 June 2026): ~£9m ($12m), term up to 10 years incl. extensions; original tender ~£17m incl. tax; competitive tender, Accenture and NEC Software lost. Eastern 'Nectar' pilot: value not disclosed (FOI refused). Procurement transparency is poor: Good Law Project FOI'd 45 forces, 35 refused citing national-security/law-enforcement exemptions.
profile.operational_vs_statistical: OPERATIONAL — Nectar is a live investigation/decision-support and real-time data-sharing platform (profiling, evidence processing, suspect mapping); the firearms platform makes live licensing decisions. This is the operational side of the line the Splink investigation drew, aligning with the operational Splink 'Master Record' (real-time, North Essex PNC feed) rather than research-only Data First.
profile.consent_oversight: Weak. Bedfordshire DPIA admits NO individual opt-out for people whose data appears, and shows consultation was 'needed' but with no evidence it occurred / no public consultation detail. Lawful basis is public-task/law-enforcement processing (DPA 2018 Part 3), so no individual consent is sought — mirroring the Splink-investigation finding (lawful but no consent/notification/opt-out). Oversight: West Midlands NDAS (Accenture, separate) used Alan Turing Institute ethics review; for Palantir/Nectar no equivalent independent ethics sign-off is documented. Parliamentary scrutiny: Commons Science, Innovation & Technology Committee called Palantir reliance an 'unacceptable point of weakness'; David Davis MP demanded scrutiny. Transparency undermined by mass FOI refusals.
controversies_litigation: Palantir signalled legal challenge against Sadiq Khan / Mayor's Office for Policing and Crime over the blocked ~£50m Met contract (May 2026); Conservative figures urged the suit.
Amnesty International described the Nectar-type system as 'indiscriminate mass surveillance'; TUC warned about trade-union membership data echoing blacklisting.
Good Law Project: 45 forces FOI'd, 35 refused on national-security grounds; Leicestershire removed its Palantir contract from public record after press inquiries.
Commons Science, Innovation and Technology Committee: reliance on Palantir an 'unacceptable point of weakness'.
Reputational baggage: Palantir's work with US ICE and the Israeli military cited by campaigners (StopWatch, Medact, CAAT) opposing UK police/NHS contracts.
Liberty Investigates / Democracy for Sale reporting on a covert 'real-time surveillance network' and how forces hide Palantir work.
datasets: Force crime/intelligence records (suspects, victims, witnesses, children, vulnerable persons)
Special-category data: race, religion, political opinions, sexual orientation/sex life, health, trade-union membership, philosophical beliefs
Financial records, social media posts, dating profiles
Seized digital-device evidence (e.g. 1.4TB phone extractions, message content)
Firearms / explosives-precursor / poisons licensing records (national, 43 forces)
NOTE: no documented ingestion of the Police National Computer (PNC) or MoJ Splink-linked datasets into Palantir
unverified_flags: '11 police forces use Palantir' is a widely-repeated figure (search-summary level) but I did not pin it to one primary source; treat as journalistic estimate. Confirmed by primary/serious reporting: Bedfordshire, Hertfordshire, Cambridgeshire, Leicestershire/EMSOU; Met (blocked).
Whether the East 'Nectar' product is branded Gotham vs Foundry per force is largely undisclosed (FOI refusals); 'Nectar' is the confirmed UK brand.
No evidence found of Palantir integrating with the PNC; the Splink North Essex PNC→police feed is a SEPARATE MoJ pipeline — any link between them is inferred geography, not documented.
Exact Nectar pilot contract value not disclosed.
Bedfordshire DPIA 11-special-categories detail relies on Bedford Independent + Liberty Investigates reporting of the DPIA, not the raw DPIA reviewed by me.

profile.caveats: High confidence on the Palantir UK-policing footprint (Nectar pilots, EMSOU/Leicestershire, firearms licensing, blocked Met deal) from serious journalism + The Register + Good Law Project. The CONNECTION TIER to the Splink entities is deliberately rated SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP, not DIRECT: there is NO documented shared pipeline between Palantir and MoJ Splink, and no documented Palantir–PNC integration. The East-of-England geographic coincidence (Nectar's Eastern region vs Splink's North Essex PNC→police feed) is suggestive of a convergent trajectory but is NOT evidence of data flowing between them. NDAS is Accenture/AWS and must not be attributed to Palantir. The '11 forces' figure is journalistic, not primary-sourced here.

overclaims: TIER OVERCLAIM (central): The profile rates the Splink link as SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP. That is itself too strong. Splink is a Ministry of Justice (MoJ) open-source library; Palantir/Nectar is deployed by territorial POLICE FORCES and Home Office/NPCC/PDS bodies. MoJ and the police/Home Office are NOT the same department, so there is no 'same-department' overlap to claim. The local authoritative evidence file /Users/btmaffiliate/uk-data-linkage-disclosure/evidence/Splink-Palantir-Connection-Evidence.md concludes 'There is no documented direct technical or corporate connection between Splink and Palantir' and that 'Palantir'/'Foundry' appear nowhere in the Splink repo, and Splink is not a Foundry backend. The honest tier is THEMATIC_ADJACENCY (both touch UK criminal-justice person-level record-linkage), NOT same-department.

GEOGRAPHIC-COINCIDENCE OVERCLAIM: 'Striking geographic adjacency' / 'flagship operational pilot ... same East-of-England space' is rhetorical inflation. Nectar is also branded 'Nectar emsou' in the East Midlands (Leicestershire/EMSOU) per Contract Finder Pro, so 'Nectar = Eastern region' is not a clean geographic signature pointing at North Essex. The Splink North Essex PNC feed is an MoJ pipeline; co-location in 'the East of England' is coincidence, not connective tissue. Drop the 'striking'/'flagship' adjacency framing.

SHARED-GOAL OVERCLAIM: Equating Nectar's 'single unified view' with Splink's 'Master Record'/'Core Person Record' as the 'same kind of person-level identity-resolution goal' implies technical convergence. Both resolve identities, but so does every record-linkage system on earth; shared generic objective is not a documented link. Frame as analogy, not connection.

PRODUCT-NAMING OVERCLAIM: 'Gotham/Nectar investigative lineage' and 'Foundry-class records system' assert Palantir product names the profile itself admits are undisclosed (FOI refused). 'Nectar' is the confirmed UK police brand; Gotham vs Foundry per force is NOT documented. The Gotham/Foundry attributions are inference and should be labelled as such, not stated as product fact.

title: Ministry of Justice / prisons / probation — any Palantir presence

profile.vector: Ministry of Justice / prisons / probation / criminal justice — Palantir presence
profile.uk_body: Ministry of Justice (MoJ) and HM Prison and Probation Service (HMPPS); adjacent bodies surfaced: UK police forces (Bedfordshire, Hertfordshire, Cambridgeshire + East Midlands/Leicestershire) and the Metropolitan Police (MOPAC)
profile.summary: There is NO documented Palantir contract, pilot, or live deployment inside the Ministry of Justice or HMPPS (prisons/probation/courts) — that absence is itself the central finding. In November 2024, FOI-released correspondence (The Guardian / Ben Quinn) showed Palantir actively LOBBIED MoJ ministers — including prisons minister James Timpson and chancellor Rachel Reeves — to deploy its analytics for prisoner reoffending-risk scoring, prison-capacity optimisation and "secure information sharing and data analytics," and Palantir attended a ~30-corporation MoJ surveillance roundtable. Ministers largely did not respond and no award followed. Crucially, the MoJ's actual operational reoffending tool (ARNS, replacing OASys, profiling 1,300+ people daily using probation/prison caseload data combined with the Police National Computer) is built IN-HOUSE by MoJ Digital with Capita — explicitly not Palantir. The only genuinely operational Palantir presence touching criminal justice is in POLICING (the "Nectar" real-time data-sharing network across East-of-England/East Midlands forces), a different body from MoJ/HMPPS even though it processes the same PNC data that Splink's operational deployment also feeds.
what_palantir_does: In MoJ/HMPPS: nothing operational. Palantir only PROPOSED (lobbying, not delivery) to score prisoners' reoffending risk, model recidivism drivers such as income and addiction, optimise finite prison capacity, and build cross-agency 'secure information sharing' — none of which was contracted.

In policing (operational, separate body): the 'Nectar' platform provides a 'single unified view' real-time data-sharing network across ~9 East-of-England forces plus a parallel 5-force East Midlands project; processes crime/financial records, suspect AND victim/witness/children data, and special-category data (political opinions, health, trade-union membership, sexual orientation, race).

Casework example (Bedfordshire, Nov 2025): Palantir 'Nectar' read/translated 100,000+ Romanian-to-English seized phone messages and link-charted movements to help convict an ATM-theft gang — an operational, not statistical, use of citizen data.

Adjacent state footprint (context, not MoJ): NHS Federated Data Platform (~£330m), MoD (£240.6m Dec 2025 + ~£1.5bn partnership), FCA fraud, Home Office, Coventry social services — The Nerve/Carole Cadwalladr counted 34+ contracts across 10+ departments, £670m+ (now >£900m). MoJ is NOT in that contract list.
profile.link_to_splink_work: SAME-DEPARTMENT OVERLAP, NOT a merged pipeline. Splink is most operational precisely in the MoJ/criminal-justice space (moj-splink-master-record: courts/sentencing, Core Person Record pilot, daily North Essex PNC→police feed). Palantir tried to enter the very same MoJ space (reoffending risk, data sharing) but FAILED to win any contract; the operational MoJ scoring tool (ARNS) is in-house (MoJ Digital + Capita), and it — not Palantir — uses the PNC data, the same PNC data Splink links. So the only concrete intersection is thematic/data-source overlap on the Police National Computer at the policing layer (Palantir 'Nectar' and Splink's North Essex PNC feed both touch PNC-derived policing data), but there is NO documented data flow between Splink and Palantir, and NO Palantir code in any Splink/Data First/Core Person Record system. The layers are adjacent (same department, same PNC data universe), not merged.
profile.contract_value_dates: MoJ/HMPPS: £0 — no contract awarded. Lobbying timeline: Nov 2024 FOI release; Palantir exec letter to prisons minister Timpson ~3 weeks after the July 2024 general election; CEO Alex Karp letters to Reeves, science minister Peter Kyle, CST Darren Jones, trade minister Douglas Alexander; talks reportedly began under the prior Conservative government; ministers did not respond. Policing (separate): Metropolitan Police up-to-£50m AI deal BLOCKED by Sadiq Khan/MOPAC on 21 May 2026 (process/value-for-money + ethics concerns; >£500k requires MOPAC sign-off); Leicestershire/East Midlands ~£800k contract reportedly de-listed from public record after press queries; Nectar pilot values undisclosed. Wider state context: NHS FDP ~£330m/7yr; MoD £240.6m (Dec 2025).
profile.operational_vs_statistical: In MoJ/HMPPS, no Palantir system exists in either mode. Where Palantir IS live (policing), it is unambiguously OPERATIONAL — real-time surveillance/intelligence (Nectar), individual-level investigation, phone-message exploitation, conviction support — contrasting with Splink's MoJ Data First research-only datasets but aligning with Splink's separately-documented operational courts/sentencing/PNC use. The MoJ in-house ARNS reoffending tool is also operational (per-individual decisions affecting parole/risk), but it is not Palantir.
profile.consent_oversight: No individual consent or opt-out documented in any of these criminal-justice uses (mirrors the Splink finding). Policing Nectar processes victim/witness/children and special-category data with a Bedfordshire DPIA itself noting public 'skepticism or resistance' over data sharing; Palantir's defence is that it ingests only data the force 'already holds.' Oversight gap is highlighted by the Met case: MOPAC found the £50m deal had bypassed the required >£500k approval/oversight process, and Leicestershire allegedly removed a listing from the public record — i.e., active opacity. For the MoJ ARNS tool, prisoners report being unaware of, or unable to meaningfully challenge, what is written about them.

title: Cross-government / Cabinet Office / ONS / National Data Library

profile.vector: Cross-government / Cabinet Office / ONS / National Data Library
profile.uk_body: Cabinet Office (Border Operations Centre); DLUHC/MHCLG (Homes for Ukraine, with Home Office + local authorities); Crown Commercial Service (framework host). NOT ONS Integrated Data Service, NOT National Fraud Initiative/PSFA, NOT (yet) National Data Library.
profile.summary: Palantir has a documented, operational cross-government data-integration footprint anchored in the Cabinet Office. Under a ~£20m (initial £7.85m + two £6.125m extensions) Border Operations Centre / "border flow" contract awarded without competitive tender in August 2020, the Cabinet Office authorised Palantir's Foundry platform to extract and combine data from seven bodies — Home Office, HMRC, Defra, Department for Transport, DVSA, Highways England and Port Health Authorities — described internally as one of the most comprehensive cross-government datasets ever compiled. A second documented cross-departmental flow is the Homes for Ukraine scheme, where Palantir's platform (6 months free, then £4.5m in Sept 2022, extended +£5.5m Sept 2023) lets DLUHC, the Home Office and 2,000+ local-authority caseworkers across England/NI share applicant data. Palantir has been a Crown Commercial Service supplier since April 2021 and lists Foundry & AIP on G-Cloud 14, the procurement on-ramp for further cross-government spread. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: the ONS Integrated Data Service is built on Google Cloud/BigQuery (not Palantir), the National Fraud Initiative/PSFA data-matching runs on Synectics Solutions (not Palantir), and the proposed National Data Library has no named Palantir role — so Palantir is the OPERATIONAL integrator at the Cabinet Office while ONS/Splink remain the STATISTICAL linkers; they are not the same pipeline.
what_palantir_does: Cabinet Office Border Operations Centre: Foundry's 'Data Connector' extracts live operational data from 7 departments' systems (Home Office and HMRC in phase 1; DfT, Defra, DVSA, Highways England, port-area local authorities in phase 2) into a single dedicated Foundry partition for risk-based monitoring of the flow/throughput of goods and people across the UK border post-Brexit.
Data hosted in a dedicated UK partition of the Palantir Foundry System on AWS London region, stated as for the exclusive use of HMG.

Homes for Ukraine: a single Foundry-based platform lets DLUHC, the Home Office and 2,000+ local-authority caseworkers share and manage end-to-end refugee/sponsor applicant data (identity, safeguarding, accommodation matching).

Operates as a 'single source of truth' integration layer — Foundry's stated function is mapping disparate source-system data to a unified customer data model/objects; this is operational case-level integration, not de-identified statistical research linkage.
Available to the wider public sector via Crown Commercial Service / G-Cloud 14 (Foundry & AIP service listing, 2024), enabling further direct-award call-offs across departments, councils and police.
profile.link_to_splink_work: SAME-DEPARTMENT / SAME-ENTITY OVERLAP, NOT a shared pipeline. The Splink investigation's entities include the Home Office and Cabinet Office. Palantir's Cabinet Office Border Operations Centre directly ingests Home Office (and HMRC) data, and its Homes for Ukraine platform fuses DLUHC + Home Office data — so Palantir and the Splink ecosystem touch the SAME departments (Home Office, Cabinet Office). BUT there is NO documented data flow between Splink and Palantir Foundry, and no shared dataset. The decisive separation: ONS's statistical linkage estate (Integrated Data Service) runs on Google Cloud/BigQuery — NOT Palantir; the National Fraud Initiative runs on Synectics — NOT Palantir; the National Data Library has no Palantir role. Splink/ONS = statistical, de-identified, research-only linkage; Palantir/Cabinet Office = operational, identifiable, case-level integration. The honest framing is two parallel 'joining-up' tracks in the same government, occasionally over the same departments, with no proven technical bridge between them.
profile.palantir_product: Foundry (with Data Connector); AIP also now listed on G-Cloud 14
profile.contract_value_dates: Cabinet Office border flow: tested Foundry Dec 2019 (free no-deal-planning trial); contract Aug 2020 — initial 1-yr £7.85m + two 1-yr extensions £6.125m each (up to ~£20m), awarded WITHOUT competitive tender (direct award justified under Public Contracts Regulations 2015; criticised by MPs incl. Clive Lewis). Homes for Ukraine (DLUHC): 6 months FREE, then 12-mo direct award £4.5m (Sept 2022), extended +£5.5m (Sept 2023) = ~£10m; the government's chief commercial officer formally wrote to Palantir noting concern that offering services at little/nominal cost to gain a commercial foothold cuts against open-competition principles. Palantir named a Crown Commercial Service supplier Apr 2021; Foundry & AIP on G-Cloud 14 (service definition dated Nov 2024) — this CCS/G-Cloud framework is the compliant on-ramp for subsequent cross-government call-offs. Byline Times/The Citizens cumulative UK public-sector spend estimate £94m (since 2009) rising to £244.5m; later (Jan 2026, The Nerve) reporting cites 34+ contracts across 10+ departments worth £670m+ government-wide (most of that is NHS FDP £330m/£500m and MoD, outside this vector).
profile.operational_vs_statistical: OPERATIONAL. Both documented Cabinet Office-vector uses are identifiable, case-level, real-time/near-real-time integration for live decision-making (border throughput risk monitoring; refugee caseworking) — not anonymised statistical/research linkage. This is the categorical opposite of ONS/Splink's statutory, de-identified, research-only model (and the opposite of MoJ Data First's 'not for operational decision-making' research datasets). It mirrors the Splink dossier's own 'Statistics or Operations?' finding: the alarming track is the operational, identifiable one — and in the cross-government space that track is Palantir, not Splink.
profile.consent_oversight: No documented individual consent/notification/opt-out for cross-departmental combination; lawful basis = departmental data-sharing agreements / public task. NAO investigation + PAC scrutiny of Homes for Ukraine; MPs (EDM 65235, Hansard) and openDemocracy/Byline Times/Foxglove raised procurement and mosaic-effect/re-identification concerns over concentrating cross-government data in one private US vendor.

profile.caveats: Cumulative spend figures vary by source and vintage (£94m → £244.5m → £670m+) because they aggregate different scopes and years; the £670m+ figure is government-wide and dominated by NHS FDP and MoD, which are outside this Cabinet-Office/ONS/NDL vector. The Cabinet Office border deal's headline value is the contracted ceiling (~£20m); actual spend if extensions were/weren't exercised is not fully confirmed here. The National Data Library is still pre-procurement (details due spring 2026), so a future Palantir role cannot be excluded — only that none is documented now. The link to the Splink investigation is department/entity overlap (Home Office, Cabinet Office), explicitly NOT a documented Splink↔Palantir data pipeline.
unverified_flags: The Nerve's £670m / 34-contracts / 10-departments tally not independently cross-checked against Contracts Finder line-by-line in this pass.
Whether the Cabinet Office border contract's two extension years were actually exercised (full ~£20m realised) not confirmed.
Exact current status of the border flow / Border Operations Centre Palantir deployment in 2026 not verified.
National Data Library design-phase contractor name not confirmed (reported as a separate six-figure / £650k award, not Palantir).

overclaims: TIER MISLABEL (the central one): the profile is headlined DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK, but that tier is only earned by the Palantir<->Cabinet Office relationship itself. For the thing this profile is actually being mined for - a Splink<->Palantir connection - the honest tier is SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP at best. The profile's own link_to_splink_work text concedes 'NO documented data flow between Splink and Palantir Foundry, and no shared dataset.' A Palantir lawyer would seize on the headline tier as implying a documented Splink linkage that does not exist. The DIRECT tier must not sit at the top of a profile whose disclosure purpose is the Splink story.

FRESHNESS / OPERATIONAL-STATUS OVERCLAIM: the profile presents Homes for Ukraine as a current, live Palantir data-fusion flow ('lets DLUHC, the Home Office and 2,000+ local-authority caseworkers share applicant data' - present tense). Reporting (techmonitor/silicon.co.uk/AOL, surfaced this pass) shows MHCLG has REPLACED the Palantir system with an in-house build and publicly states it is 'saving millions a year' in running costs. The Palantir HfU deployment is wound down / superseded, not an ongoing operational flow. Every present-tense HfU claim and the 'what_palantir_does' HfU bullet must be re-tensed and caveated.

NAMED-BODY ERROR (DVSA vs DVLA): the profile lists 'DVSA' among the border-flow bodies. The PublicTechnology source's free-trial list names 'DVLA' (Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency); the phase-2 invitation list separately mentions DVSA. The two are conflated. As a contract-fact claim about which bodies' data was ingested, this needs the exact agency pinned to the exact phase, or it is an inaccuracy a respondent can use to discredit the dataset list.

'SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH' / 'most comprehensive cross-government dataset ever compiled': this characterisation is sourced to campaigner/journalistic framing (openDemocracy/Byline), not to the contract or a government statement. It should be attributed as a claim ('described by openDemocracy as...'), not stated as established fact.

Cumulative spend laddering (94m -> 244.5m -> 670m): self-flagged in caveats but still presented in the summary as if cumulative. The 670m+ is government-wide and dominated by NHS FDP/MoD which the profile itself says are OUTSIDE this vector - so it should not appear in a Cabinet-Office-vector profile except as an explicit out-of-scope footnote. As written it inflates the apparent size of the Cabinet Office footprint.
strongest_sourced_claims: Border flow contract values and structure: initial 1-yr 7.85m + two optional 1-yr extensions at 6.125m each (>20m ceiling), Cabinet Office, Aug 2020, Foundry + Data Connector, near-real-time cross-department border flow data - verified against PublicTechnology (2020-10-28).

Data-hosting/data-flow claim: 'separate dedicated partition of the Palantir Foundry System for the exclusive use of HMG, hosted in the AWS London region', Palantir barred from processing data outside UK without Cabinet Office authorisation, encryption at rest/in transit - verified VERBATIM from the contract via PublicTechnology. This is the strongest data-flow claim in the profile.

Homes for Ukraine figures: 6 months free + 12-mo direct award 4.5m (Sept 2022) + 12-mo extension 5.5m (Sept 2023) - directly confirmed by the National Audit Office investigation, which names Palantir explicitly. Gold-standard primary source.

CCO rebuke: Gareth Rhys Williams (govt chief commercial officer) wrote in Feb 2023 noting concern over offering services at 'zero or nominal cost to gain a commercial foothold, contrary to the principles of public procurement which usually require open competition' - verified near-verbatim (thestack/techmonitor).

Negative-control distinctions hold up: NFI data-matching runs on Synectics Solutions, NOT Palantir (verified - Synectics incumbent since 1996, Cabinet Office re-award confirmed via UKAuthority/Synectics). ONS IDS on Google Cloud/BigQuery and NDL having no named Palantir role are correctly stated as separators.

The decisive negative claim - NO documented Splink<->Palantir technical or data pipeline - is independently corroborated by the repo's own evidence file (/Users/btmaffiliate/uk-data-linkage-disclosure/evidence/Splink-Palantir-Connection-Evidence.md): Palantir/Foundry appear nowhere in the Splink repo; no Palantir contributors; the only connective tissue is indirect via PwC's UK alliance, and even that does not touch Splink.

verification.notes: Adversarial verdict: the profile is MIXED, not SOLID, but for honesty reasons rather than fabrication. Every hard contract fact I could test (border flow 7.85m+6.125mx2/AWS-London-partition/Foundry; HfU 4.5m+5.5m+6mo-free via NAO; CCO rebuke; NFI=Synectics) checked out, several verbatim. The profile is also commendably self-aware: it explicitly refuses to claim a Splink<->Palantir pipeline, and that refusal is independently backed by the repo's own evidence log (evidence/Splink-Palantir-Connection-Evidence.md, lines 44-54). The reasons it is not SOLID: (1) the headline tier DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK is true of Palantir<->Cabinet Office but misleading when the profile's reason-for-existence is the Splink story, where the real tier is SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP - this is the single biggest publication risk and why I downgraded the adjusted tier; (2) a freshness error - Homes for Ukraine Palantir is superseded by an in-house build, yet stated in the present tense; (3) a named-body conflation (DVLA/DVSA); (4) inflation risk from the 670m government-wide figure bleeding into a Cabinet-Office-vector profile. Splink<->Palantir is THEMATIC ADJACENCY at most (shared 'joining-up' theme) with SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP on Home Office/Cabinet Office; there is no technical or data bridge and none should ever be implied. Before publication: re-tier, re-tense HfU, fix DVLA/DVSA, ring-fence the 670m figure, and attribute the 'most comprehensive dataset' rhetoric.
