profile.vector: Palantir corporate / governance / national-security dimension — who controls Palantir, its UK revenue/contract totals, procurement-transparency and direct-award controversies, lobbying/revolving-door, and the civil-liberties stakes of a US national-security firm holding integrated UK citizen data.
profile.uk_body: Palantir Technologies UK Ltd (UK subsidiary of Palantir Technologies Inc., NYSE: PLTR), counterparty to NHS England, Ministry of Defence, Home Office, multiple UK police forces, Cabinet Office / Crown Commercial Service, AWE (nuclear), Coventry City Council, DEFRA, and the Homes for Ukraine scheme.
profile.summary: Palantir is a US national-security data-integration firm, founded 2003 by Peter Thiel and Alex Karp with early CIA In-Q-Tel funding (~$2m, 2004), now ~$4.5bn revenue (FY2025) with 54% from government, and founder voting control locked at ~49.99% via a tri-class (A/B/F) share structure and a Thiel-Karp-Cohen voting trust. In the UK it holds at least £670m in disclosed contracts across 10+ government bodies, headlined by the £330m (initially £182m) NHS England Federated Data Platform (Nov 2023) and a ~£240m MoD strategic partnership (announced 2024, contract Dec 2025) awarded without competitive tender. Its core products — Foundry and Gotham — ingest, integrate and link previously siloed datasets into a single operational view, which is the same data-linkage capability at the heart of the Splink investigation, but delivered as a proprietary US-owned platform rather than open-source government tooling. The connection to the Splink-investigation entities is SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP plus thematic data-linkage adjacency: Palantir contracts with several of the same departments (NHS England, Home Office, MoD, police), but there is NO documented technical pipeline linking Splink (MoJ's open-source probabilistic record-linkage library) to Palantir Foundry/Gotham. The dominant story is governance and civil liberties: opaque direct awards, a 30+ official revolving door into the company, and a US national-security vendor positioned to hold integrated UK citizen data.
what_palantir_does: NHS England Federated Data Platform (FDP): Foundry-based platform that ingests daily hospital data (admissions, inpatient, discharge, outpatient activity) plus personal identifiers (dates of birth, postcodes, detailed medical histories) previously held separately by individual trusts, creating a near-real-time linked operational view across all English NHS hospitals (rolled out to all trusts by March 2023; FDP contract Nov 2023). NHS England reports operational gains (e.g. ~114 extra elective surgeries/month, ~35% reduction in delayed discharge days at adopting trusts).

Origin in COVID-19 Data Store (2020): first NHS deployment of Foundry under a £1 contract awarded without tender; Matt Hancock used special ministerial powers (COPI notices) to bypass patient confidentiality rules during the pandemic — the on-ramp for all later NHS work.

Core capability is data linkage and integration: Palantir's platforms are explicitly designed to move data across systems and link multiple datasets into a single unified view — the same joining-up function as government record-linkage, but proprietary and US-controlled.

UK policing 'Nectar' real-time data-sharing network (Liberty Investigates / i, June 2025): proof-of-concept linking crime records with financial information and other intelligence across forces (Bedfordshire, Leicestershire confirmed; Hertfordshire/Cambridgeshire piloting; ~9 forces plus East Midlands). Processes data on suspects, victims, witnesses, children and vulnerable people, and special-category data (political opinions, beliefs, health, trade-union membership, sexual orientation, race). Palantir also providing AI investigation tools to ~11 police forces.

MoD: ~£388m in current/historic contracts (12+ awards), culminating in a ~£240m strategic data-analytics partnership for strategic/tactical/live operational decision-making across armed forces — >3x any prior MoD award, not competitively tendered.

Home Office / immigration: documented UK Home Office contracts; internationally Palantir runs ICE's Investigative Case Management system (since 2014), the FALCON/Gotham implementation, and a ~$30m 'ImmigrationOS' deportation-targeting platform (April 2025) — the international template for what integrated immigration data linkage looks like under Palantir.

Other UK bodies: ~£15m AWE Nuclear Security Technologies 'cloud support' (not on Contracts Finder; MoD declined to confirm), ~£250k Nuclear Propulsion IPT, Cabinet Office Foundry use for Brexit border planning, plus Coventry City Council, DEFRA and the Homes for Ukraine scheme.
profile.link_to_splink_work: SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP plus THEMATIC_ADJACENCY — and this distinction must be kept honest. (1) Same departments: Palantir holds documented contracts with bodies that also feature in the Splink investigation — NHS England (FDP), Home Office, MoD, and UK police forces. (2) Same theme: both Splink (MoJ Data First's open-source probabilistic record-linkage library) and Palantir Foundry/Gotham exist to JOIN previously siloed citizen datasets into a linked whole — Palantir is effectively the proprietary, US-owned, operational commercial analogue of the open-source government data-linkage work Splink represents. (3) Critical caveat — NO documented technical link: there is no published contract, data-flow, or pipeline showing Splink running inside, feeding, or being fed by Palantir, nor any evidence the MoJ Data First Splink work uses Palantir. The DIRECT documented overlap is institutional (shared departments) and trajectory (the 'quiet joining-up' of citizen data), NOT a shared engineering pipeline. Do not assert a Splink↔Palantir technical integration; none is evidenced.
profile.palantir_product: Primarily Foundry (NHS FDP, COVID-19 Data Store, Cabinet Office Brexit border planning, commercial/MoD analytics) and Gotham (intelligence/defence/policing operational platform; ICE FALCON internationally). AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) is Palantir's newer LLM-orchestration layer increasingly bundled with both. UK policing 'Nectar' uses a Palantir platform (Gotham-class) though the specific SKU was not named in the source.
profile.contract_value_dates: At least £670m total disclosed UK government contracts (The Nerve investigation). Breakdown: MoD ~£388m (12+ contracts/extensions, bulk from 2018; earliest a 2012 Olympics contract); NHS ~£244m across 12 contracts/extensions; AWE Nuclear ~£15m (cloud support, off Contracts Finder); Nuclear Propulsion IPT ~£250k. Headline awards: NHS FDP — published award £182.2m, envelope up to £330m over 7 years, awarded Nov 2023, initial 3-year term ending Feb 2027 (break clause); FDP delivery partners Accenture, PwC, NECS, Carnall Farrar. MoD strategic partnership ~£240m, 3-year, announced 2024 / contracted Dec 2025, NOT competitively tendered. Original NHS COVID-19 Data Store: £1 (2020), extended through Jan 2023, no figure initially published. Procurement route: mix of competitive (FDP) and direct/no-tender awards (COVID £1 contract, MoD £240m, AWE contracts absent from Contracts Finder), with persistent transparency gaps — many of the 34 identified contracts not on the official Contracts Finder site.
profile.operational_vs_statistical: Overwhelmingly OPERATIONAL, not statistical. Unlike the ONS/MoJ Data First/Splink lineage (de-identified, accredited-researcher, statistical-research framing under safe-settings governance), Palantir's UK deployments are live operational tooling: the FDP drives day-to-day hospital operations (bed/discharge/elective management), the MoD contract supports 'strategic, tactical and live operational decision-making,' and the police Nectar network is real-time intelligence sharing. This is the key contrast — Splink-type work is statistical record-linkage for research; Palantir is real-time operational data integration acting on identifiable or re-identifiable individuals.
profile.consent_oversight: Weak and contested. NHS England relies on pseudonymisation rather than consent; an NHS document acknowledges the company will 'collect and process confidential patient information,' and legal experts note pseudonymised health data is easily re-identified. There is no clear patient opt-out mapped to FDP; the COVID origin used emergency COPI notices to bypass confidentiality. Foxglove (Cori Crider) acted for the National Pensioners' Convention, Just Treatment and the Doctors Association UK, writing to NHS England over pseudonymised-data sharing; NHS England did not substantively answer consent concerns after months. BMA passed motions opposing FDP rollout on governance/ethics grounds and urged doctors to limit engagement. Police Nectar special-category data processing raised data-breach and predictive-policing oversight concerns. MoD/AWE awards lack competitive-tender transparency. Critics (Lib Dem MP Martin Wrigley) warn of 'massive technical lock-in' weakening democratic oversight.
controversies_litigation: openDemocracy/Foxglove litigation forcing publication of the secret 2020 COVID-19 Data Store contract and challenging the no-tender extension.

Good Law Project 'Say No to Palantir' / 'No Palantir in the NHS' campaign — ~50,000 patients wrote to NHS trust boards; coalition including Privacy International, Corporate Watch, Medact, backed by Amnesty International; Foxglove legal challenges over pseudonymised data sharing.

Revolving door: investigations (The Nerve) found Palantir hired 30+ senior UK officials, including the AI strategy leaders of both the NHS and the MoD. Named: Indra Joshi (ex-NHSX director of AI, who helped build the COVID-19 datastore → Palantir director of health/research/AI, April 2022); Harjeet Dhaliwal (ex-deputy director of data services, NHS England); from MoD — Barnaby Kistruck (helped draft the Strategic Defence Review, joined 9 days after leaving MoD), Laurence Lee, Damian Parmenter, Polly Scully (ex-MoD strategic director), and ex-armed-forces minister Leo Docherty.

Governance/control: tri-class share structure (Class A 1 vote, Class B 10 votes, Class F variable) keeps the Thiel-Karp-Cohen founder voting trust at ~49.99% in perpetuity while founders hold a minority economic stake; flagged in S-1 and shareholder litigation.

Foreign-control / data-sovereignty stakes: a US firm whose chairman Peter Thiel is a major Trump donor and which holds ICE deportation contracts being positioned to hold integrated UK NHS/policing/defence data; fears (raised in The Lowdown and campaigners) that Palantir's linkage capability could enable Home Office/police access to confidential patient data.
Louis Mosley (Palantir EVP UK & Europe), grandson of Oswald Mosley, appeared at the right-wing ARC conference (2025) alongside Nigel Farage and Jordan Peterson — reputational/lobbying optics.
MoD £240m award without competitive tender criticised by Action on Armed Violence (Iain Overton) and Lib Dem MP Martin Wrigley over military-industrial revolving door and vendor lock-in.
datasets: NHS England hospital activity data (admissions, inpatient, discharge, outpatient) plus identifiers: dates of birth, postcodes, detailed medical histories (FDP / Faster Data Flows)
NHS COVID-19 Data Store datasets (2020 origin)
Prospective FDP scope reportedly including GP and social care records (contested / not fully confirmed)

UK police crime records linked with financial information and other intelligence; special-category data (political opinions, beliefs, health, trade-union membership, sexual orientation, race) on suspects, victims, witnesses, children, vulnerable people (Nectar)
MoD strategic/tactical/operational data across security classifications
Home Office immigration data (UK); internationally ICE federal/private database networks (FALCON/ICM/ImmigrationOS)
Cabinet Office Brexit border-planning data
unverified_flags: The FDP including GP and social care records is reported/expected but not confirmed as live ingestion.
The specific Palantir product SKU behind the police 'Nectar' network was not named in the primary source (described as Gotham-class).

'34 contracts / 10 departments' and the '£670m total' come from journalistic aggregation (The Nerve) given gaps in Contracts Finder; exact totals depend on what is counted and are not fully reconcilable to official disclosure.
FY2025 ~$4.5bn revenue and 54% government split are drawn from press summaries of Palantir filings; treat the exact split as approximate pending direct 10-K confirmation.
No documented technical link between MoJ Data First's Splink and Palantir; any such linkage would be SPECULATIVE and is explicitly excluded.
profile.caveats: This profile establishes a strong institutional and thematic relationship to the Splink-investigation entities (shared departments; same 'join up citizen data' trajectory) but deliberately does NOT assert a technical pipeline between Splink and Palantir, because none is documented. Splink is open-source statistical record-linkage tooling owned by the MoJ; Palantir Foundry/Gotham is proprietary operational data integration owned by a US national-security firm — they are conceptual cousins, not a proven shared system. Many UK contract figures rest on investigative aggregation due to genuine procurement-transparency gaps, so totals are best-estimate floors, not audited figures. Financials are from secondary summaries of SEC filings and should be confirmed against the primary 10-K before publication.

As a hostile critic I cannot refute the institutional overlap (Palantir and the Splink-investigation entities genuinely share NHS England, Home Office, MoD, and police as counterparties), and the profile already declines to claim more than that. As a Palantir lawyer, the litigable risk in this profile is low precisely because it self-limits the Splink link to SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP + THEMATIC_ADJACENCY and explicitly disclaims any technical pipeline — and my independent searching confirms no such pipeline is documented. The contract/financial/governance/revolving-door claims are unusually well-sourced and survive scrutiny; the only defensible attacks are the four overclaims above (COVID extension figure understated, Joshi datastore wording, and two single-source nuclear figures presented with insufficient attribution). Tier stays SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP. Before publication: keep the Splink no-technical-link disclaimer, attribute the £670m and AWE/nuclear figures to The Nerve as investigative floors, add the £23m COVID figure, and soften the Joshi datastore phrasing. title: The analytical distinction — Splink vs Palantir, and any direct data-flow

profile.vector: The analytical distinction — Splink vs Palantir — and whether any direct data-flow exists between them. Splink is an open-source probabilistic record-linkage LIBRARY (a Python package) built by the MoJ; it links records but is not itself a data platform, has no network listener, and runs inside whatever environment an analyst puts it in. Palantir Foundry/Gotham are PROPRIETARY, hosted operational data-integration platforms that ingest, fuse, and action live data. They are different categories of artefact: a linkage algorithm vs an operational platform. The honest answer to the leads question is: there is NO documented case of them being the same pipeline, and NO documented flow of Splink-linked or ONS/Data-First data INTO a Palantir platform (or vice versa). The real link is institutional and trajectory-based, not technical.
profile.uk_body: Multiple — most relevant to the Splink investigation: Ministry of Justice (Palantir 2024 reoffending-risk pitch; Splink's home dept), NHS England (Federated Data Platform, £330m), plus Home Office, MoD (£240m), Cabinet Office/Border Operations Centre (£27m), UK police (East of England "Nectar"/East Midlands), AWE nuclear (£15m). DWP/HMRC referenced via the broader fraud-data trajectory but no confirmed Palantir person-level operational deal there.
profile.palantir_product: Foundry (NHS FDP, Cabinet Office border tool, MoJ pitch, police "Nectar") and Gotham (defence/intelligence, MoD). Foundry↔Gotham are documented as interoperable ("drag and drop" data transfer) but that is a CAPABILITY, not evidence of a specific cross-domain flow.
what_palantir_does: NHS Federated Data Platform (£330m, 7-yr, awarded Nov 2023; Accenture/PwC/NECS/Carnall Farrar subcontractors): joins fragmented NHS operational data — elective waiting lists, bed occupancy, theatre rosters, supply chain — into a single OPERATIONAL view for direct care and planning. Identifiable data is held in a trust's local instance for direct care (no opt-out for direct care); de-identification (IQVIA) occurs when shared up to ICB/national level. NHS England states the supplier cannot access/use/share data for its own purposes.

MoJ (2024): Palantir lobbied the Ministry of Justice — and ministers including Chancellor Rachel Reeves — offering to calculate prisoners' 'reoffending risks', analyse prison capacity, and aggregate state-held data to identify reoffending trends (drivers such as income/addiction). Revealed via FOI. This was a PITCH/discussion, not a signed contract or live deployment. Amnesty International warned of discrimination, privacy breaches and miscarriages of justice.

Cabinet Office / Border Operations Centre (£27m, Aug 2020, no competitive tender; preceded by free Foundry trial and a July 2019 Karp–Manzoni intro brokered by ex-MI6 chief John Sawers): permission to COMBINE data from Home Office, Defra, HMRC, DfT, DVSA, Highways England and Port Health Authority into one cross-government view — described as among the most comprehensive cross-government datasets ever compiled.

UK police: Foundry-based 'Nectar' real-time data-sharing network across East of England forces (Bedfordshire, Hertfordshire, Cambridgeshire; ~9 forces, ambition to go national) and an East Midlands project (Leicestershire + 5 forces), reported operational 16 June 2025. Fuses crime records with financial/intelligence data AND special-category data (political opinions, philosophical beliefs, health, trade-union membership, sexual orientation, race) plus records on victims, children, witnesses and vulnerable people. Palantir claimed it flagged 120+ young people at risk in its first 8 days. Critics call it predictive policing / mass surveillance.

MoD: £240m data-analytics/AI contract (Dec 2025, no competitive tender) for strategic, tactical and live operational decision-making across classifications; prior £75.2m (Nov 2022) and first £28m (2018). AWE nuclear-weapons agency £15m (revealed Jan 2026).

Total documented UK state contracts: at least £670m across 10+ departments/councils/police (The Nerve, Jan 2026), with some tallies ~£600m-£900m. On 3 June 2026 the cross-party Commons Science, Innovation and Technology Committee called for termination of the NHS contract; renewal/break point February 2027.
profile.link_to_splink_work: NOT a shared pipeline. There is NO documented technical integration or data-sharing flow between Splink (or Splink-linked Data First / ONS IDS outputs) and any Palantir platform — neither Palantir's own Foundry data-integration docs nor the campaigner/journalist briefings reference Splink, Data First or ONS record-linkage in connection with Palantir, and I found no contract, DUA or architecture document tying them. The genuine connection is twofold and institutional: (1) SAME DEPARTMENT — Splink is the MoJ's own open-source linkage tool (powering Data First research datasets and the operational North Essex probation/PNC feed), and in 2024 Palantir pitched the SAME MoJ to compute prisoners' 'reoffending risks' over state-held data. They would operate on overlapping subject-matter (reoffending/justice data) within one department, but via entirely separate tooling and with no proven common pipeline. (2) SAME TRAJECTORY — both sit on the statistics→operations gradient central to the Splink dossier: Splink-linked Data First is nominally research-only yet the same library runs operationally (courts, daily PNC feeds); Palantir's platforms are explicitly OPERATIONAL/identifiable (NHS direct care, police real-time, MoD live decisions). Palantir is the operational end-state that the Splink investigation warned record-linkage could feed toward — a thematic and departmental adjacency, not a wired data flow. Manufacturing a Splink→Palantir data link would be unsupported by the evidence.
profile.operational_vs_statistical: Sharp contrast and this is the analytical crux. Splink/Data First/ONS IDS are positioned at the STATISTICAL/research end (Data First datasets are MoJ-ATR-flagged 'not for operational decision-making'; ONS uses Splink for Census/Business/Demographic indices) — though the SAME Splink tool also runs operationally in places (North Essex PNC→police feed, courts). Palantir's UK deployments are overwhelmingly at the OPERATIONAL/identifiable end: NHS FDP for live care coordination and hospital flow (opt-out impossible for direct care), police real-time fusion of identifiable special-category data, Cabinet Office live border flow, MoD live operational/tactical decision-making. Palantir is the operational pole; Splink/ONS are nearer the statistical pole — the two are not the same system.
profile.consent_oversight: See consent_oversight field above — lawful public-task basis, no individual consent/opt-out for NHS direct care, contested transparency, multiple no-tender awards, now under active parliamentary scrutiny (Commons SIT Committee termination call, Jun 2026).
profile.contract_value_dates: NHS FDP: £330m, 7-yr, awarded Nov 2023 (first-year ≥£25.6m; ~£480m cited including associated services); review/break Feb 2027. MoD: £240m (Dec 2025, no tender); £75.2m (Nov 2022); £28m (2018). Cabinet Office/Border Operations Centre: £27m (Aug 2020, no tender). AWE nuclear: £15m (revealed Jan 2026). Police Nectar/East Midlands: value undisclosed, operational 16 Jun 2025. Aggregate documented: ≥£670m across 10+ bodies (The Nerve, Jan 2026). MoJ reoffering work: a 2024 PITCH only — no contract value, not awarded.
controversies_litigation: Commons Science, Innovation and Technology Committee called for termination of the NHS FDP contract (3 June 2026); UK govt launched a review / weighing the break clause (Apr–Jun 2026).
Amnesty International 'No Palantir in our NHS' campaign; Medact, Foxglove and Good Law Project sustained legal/advocacy pressure over the NHS contract and data governance.

Liberty Investigates / i exposé of the East of England police 'Nectar' real-time surveillance network processing special-category data on victims, children and witnesses; David Davis MP demanded parliamentary scrutiny; characterised by rights groups as predictive policing / mass surveillance.
MoJ reoffending-risk pitch (2024) drew Amnesty warnings of discrimination and miscarriages of justice.
openDemocracy: Cabinet Office border contract won after ex-MI6 chief introduction, no competitive tender; Palantir hired four ex-MoD officials before the £240m defence win.

Concerns over ties to Peter Thiel / Louis Mosley and US data-sovereignty / surveillance reputation; majority of NHS hospital trusts had not adopted FDP by end-2024 (rollout failures per Corporate Watch FOIs).
datasets: NHS operational data: elective waiting lists, bed occupancy, theatre rosters, medical supply chain, patient records (identifiable at trust level for direct care; de-identified when shared to ICB/national)

Police: crime/PNC records fused with financial and intelligence data plus special-category data (political opinions, philosophical beliefs, health, trade-union membership, sexual orientation, race) and records on victims, children, witnesses, vulnerable people
Cabinet Office border: combined Home Office, Defra, HMRC, DfT, DVSA, Highways England, Port Health Authority data
MoJ (pitched only): state-held reoffending/prison-capacity data
MoD/AWE: classified strategic/tactical/operational defence and nuclear-deterrent data
NOTE: NO documented Splink-linked Data First dataset or ONS Integrated Data Service dataset is shown flowing into any Palantir platform.
unverified_flags: DWP/HMRC: a Palantir person-level OPERATIONAL fraud/welfare contract is implied by the broader trajectory and the Cabinet Office HMRC-data permission, but I found no confirmed signed Palantir-DWP welfare-data deal — treat as unverified.
Aggregate UK contract value ranges (£600m / £670m / up to £900m) vary by source and counting method — £670m (The Nerve, Jan 2026) is the best-documented floor.
Foundry↔Gotham 'drag and drop' interoperability is a documented CAPABILITY; no source proves a specific cross-domain (e.g. NHS→Home Office/enforcement) data flow actually occurred.
Exact NHS FDP headline figure cited as both £330m (core) and ~£480m (with associated services) — disambiguate when citing.
MoJ reoffending engagement: confirmed as lobbying/discussion via FOI; any progression to a contract is unverified.
profile.summary: Palantir is deeply embedded in UK government but its tooling is categorically different from Splink: Palantir Foundry/Gotham are proprietary OPERATIONAL data-fusion platforms (NHS FDP £330m/Nov 2023; MoD £240m/Dec 2025; Cabinet Office border £27m/2020; police 'Nectar' real-time network live Jun 2025; AWE nuclear £15m), whereas Splink is an open-source MoJ record-linkage library used mainly for statistics/research (ONS Census, Data First). The honest, rigorous answer to the core question is that NO documented technical pipeline or data-sharing flow connects Splink (or Splink-linked Data First / ONS IDS outputs) to any Palantir platform — that link does not exist in the public record and should not be manufactured. The real connection is institutional and trajectory-based: Palantir and Splink both operate inside the Ministry of Justice (Splink runs reoffending/Data First linkage; Palantir pitched the MoJ in 2024 to compute prisoners' 'reoffending risks'), and Palantir embodies the operational end-state — identifiable, real-time, action-oriented — toward which the Splink dossier warned record-linkage could be pulled. I therefore tier this SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP (anchored at the MoJ) with strong THEMATIC_ADJACENCY on the statistics→operations gradient, not a DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK.
profile.caveats: Tiered SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP rather than DIRECT_DOCUMENTED_LINK precisely because no shared Splink↔Palantir pipeline is documented; the MoJ overlap (Splink in-house + Palantir's 2024 reoffending pitch) and the statistics→operations trajectory are real, but the technical data flow between them is NOT and must not be asserted. Several figures are source-dependent (see unverified_flags). Some sourcing leans on advocacy/journalism (Medact, Liberty Investigates, The Nerve, openDemocracy) corroborated by NHS England's own pages, Hansard and SEC-context; campaigner framings of harm are contested by NHS England's official statements.

overclaims: Citation date error: the profile repeatedly attributes the aggregate UK-contract figure to 'The Nerve, Jan 2026'. The Nerve investigation (Carole Cadwalladr) was published FEBRUARY 2026, not January. The £15m AWE contract is cited as 'revealed Jan 2026' but is part of the same Feb 2026 reporting — verify and correct the date or it weakens every aggregate citation.

Aggregate-value incoherence: the profile presents '≥£670m across 10+ bodies' as the documented floor while also itemising the MoD £240m (Dec 2025) contract. The £670m figure PREDATES the £240.6m MoD Dec 2025 deal; sources now put the documented total >£900m. As written, the headline ≥£670m either excludes a contract the profile itself lists (internally inconsistent) or risks double-counting. The £900m upper range is the post-MoD figure, not merely a looser 'tally'.

MoD figure inconsistency: contract_value_dates lists MoD as £240m + £75.2m + £28m, but The Nerve reports MoD aggregate ~£388m across a dozen contracts/extensions. The three cited line-items are real but do not equal the documented MoD total — do not imply the three figures are the complete MoD picture.

Foundry<->Gotham 'drag and drop' interoperability is asserted as 'documented' but the profile's own citation is only the generic Foundry data-integration overview page, which describes data connection/lineage generically and does not document a Foundry-to-Gotham 'drag and drop' transfer specifically. This phrasing should be downgraded to 'general data-integration capability' or sourced more precisely.

NHS '~£480m including associated services' and 'first-year >=£25.6m' figures are stated without a dedicated citation; the only sourced NHS figure verified is the £330m / 7-yr / Nov 2023 headline. The Nerve reports NHS-related deals >£244m across 12 contracts, which does not obviously reconcile with either £330m or £480m — flag and source or drop the secondary figures.

verification.notes: VERDICT RATIONALE: The profile's connection_tier of SAME_DEPARTMENT_OVERLAP is CORRECT and should NOT be upgraded — I confirm it should not be downgraded to pure THEMATIC_ADJACENCY either, because the MoJ anchor is genuine (Splink is the MoJ's in-house tool; Palantir demonstrably pitched the same MoJ in 2024). However, as the hostile critic / Palantir lawyer, I stress the SAME_DEPARTMENT overlap is the ONLY thing elevating this above thematic: the two operate on overlapping subject-matter within one department but via entirely separate tooling with NO common pipeline. The Splink<->Palantir technical/data link is correctly characterised as non-existent and well-defended — this is the dossier's analytical strength, not a weakness, and nothing in my searches contradicts it; do not let editing pressure push it toward a manufactured DIRECT link. The MIXED verdict is driven NOT by the core analysis (which is sound) but by sloppy citation hygiene on the aggregate contract values and dates (Jan vs Feb 2026; £670m vs £900m double-counting tension; uncited NHS/MoD secondary figures; over-stated Foundry<->Gotham 'drag and drop' sourcing). These must be fixed before publication. The qualitative contract claims (NHS £330m, Cabinet Office £27m/no-tender/Sawers, police Nectar special-category data, MoJ pitch-not-contract, SIT Committee termination call) all independently verified. Confidence in the institutional/trajectory framing: high. Confidence in the numeric aggregate as currently worded: low until corrected.
